speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 2, the interview with Magda Blau. Magda, you were talking about being picked up. Who were you picked up by?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the Hlinka Guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who where they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came-- they came in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they Czech? Who are the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Slovaks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slovaks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Under whose orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the Hlinka Guard top man, which wanted me to be his girlfriend. And I said, you are my-- our neighbor. You have five children. And why you need me for a girlfriend? But I like you. So I said, I'm sorry. I don't understand those things. A father of five children to pick me for a girlfriend. So he paid me back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you taken when you were first picked up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was take to the city hall. And there was gathered all the young girls. And when the young girls wasn't-- they was in hiding, they took their fathers there. But because I was affiliated with the state school, so I-- I knew if anything happens, I will get a exemption, so I wasn't worried, which I really-- my father went there to the inspector [INAUDIBLE] they call it. And they issued such a exemption paper that I had to stay. But I tell you what. People are very terrible. There was an [INAUDIBLE]. It is painful that another Jewish girl, who was a religious instruction teacher, she went and bought my papers. She paid 20,000 crowns for the man who was there in the office and give me the-- the papers of [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that that had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my father went to check the-- with the paper. He said, the papers are not here. I say-- he said, where are they?

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, if you give me 20,000, then I take away the paper from her and give it to you. But he didn't have 20,000 crowns. So he was crying. He came to the city hall, because they wasn't allowed to come in, people. So-- to the window and he was crying like a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to a friend, they tore the-- the nicest flower out from my garden. That's how he-- he put it. But I said, don't worry. I will be all right. I will.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could speak to him at that stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. But for the time being, we-- they took us with the train. 'Shh-shh-shh-shh' train, yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Train or tram?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a train that you go to the city, where you go from one town--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they took us with the normal train. Just adding to show my personality, so I had beautiful clothes. First of all, I was a kindergarten teacher. Second of all, the dressmaker, for a long time she lived at our place, because a single girl shouldn't live in a strange town. It wasn't done. So-- and I was her model, so I had the nicest dresses. And I was the only daughter, so my mother and I also. So-- and now I said, if we are going to the butter factory, we are going to show them that we are working, but we dress up and so. So I took all my clothes. And the girls who came from the villages, they was dressed like village girls. I don't know if you know, but they have one skirt over the other, about five skirts. And then here just a shirt and like a-- a little vest. Peasant girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought, I have here the long dresses. Why should they look like-- so I started to divide all my dresses. I went to the toilet, leave this-- the things here and put on. Try one, which one you like, it's yours. How many girls were there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- they wasn't with me. They came to the transport. So it was about 12 girls I dressed up, so many dresses I had. What I had, I stayed. I left-- the-- how you say? Where you put the-- where you put the things in?

speaker: interviewer
text: The suitcase?

speaker: interviewee
text: The suitcase. I left the suitcase. I went there. And with us came the Czech gendarme, not-- they wasn't Hlinka Guard. He couldn't-- he couldn't get over-- he never met somebody who will give away all the dresses, just because to dress up her countryman. And he said that he will try to take me out, because his uncle is in the ministry. And when we arrived to the place, it was named Poprad. Then it-- it didn't look nice already.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Poprad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poprad, P-O-P-R-A-D. Poprad. Then it didn't look very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like? Describe it. It-- it was, you know, a-- it was mish-mash there, you know? It wasn't like somebody who is continuing to the-- to the butter factory. It was nobody-- nobody knew. Nobody was directing. It was a chaos. So he said, if you stay here another day, I am sending a telegram to my uncle. He should send me a telegram back, that you are exempted. To my bad luck, the same day we went. And there was waiting the cattle truck and they put us in. So then I knew. So you-- you were taken into a cattle truck. How many people in the truck with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the fullest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what it was like in that truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what it was like in the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was no room just to sit and no facilities for toilet facilities, just one little bucket. And people were suffocated there. And so much-- if they had some food they ate. Who didn't bring food, so I shared, because my mother baked and cooked, so I should have that, so I shared. And then, I knew that in one place which we are passing, not far from Poprad is a place where the Jewish boys are like laboring, not soldiers anymore the-- so I asked the girls to lift me up, because there wasn't a window in that, just-- just a small-- and that was also wired. But they lifted me up and I just saw there somebody from my town. And I screamed in Hebrew, they're taking us in the truck, in the cattle trucks. We don't know where. Please inquire, find out, and be careful. After the war, he told me he remembered my message and he tried to give the message further on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in the-- how long did it take in the cattle truck, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- I can't remember really exactly, because all the time while I was there, I occupied myself to quieten those people down and to attend to this one. You know, I-- I [LAUGHS] took over the-- the first aid like.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the atmosphere like in the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still said, if we will keep together and we will help each other, it won't be so bad, I said. But then when we arrived to Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before you arrive-- we won't get to Auschwitz yet-- I want to know a bit more about what people were saying in the trucks, how-- how they felt.

speaker: interviewee
text: They was very stressed and there was crying. And it was very uncomfortable, because they couldn't lie down and they didn't have anything to drink, anything to eat. And the facilities, to toilet facilities, it was very, very depressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it smell like in the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it smell like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. So many people together and no proper facilities. Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you can't remember how long you were in the truck. But do you know if it was more than a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Must have been more, about two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: About two days.

speaker: interviewee
text: About two days. I don't know. Really, I don't know why I forgot this part, how long it took.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that some people suffocated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- they died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No. No, no. No. They didn't die. No. Just they were-- because most of them there was young woman. This was a-- a transport with young, unmarried woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with them, I-- you know, on the same level. I said, don't worry and don't panic and don't-- you will see. We will hold together and-- and we will be all right. OK. So you get to the end of the journey. Where-- where do you come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived there. We didn't know what it-- where it is. The SS mans open the door and the dogs were barking. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH]. It was lucky for me. I didn't have any luggage, so I just had what I was, the coat, the bag, and a muff. So I jump down, because I was very sport-minded. But some girls had their luggage, you know, and they thought--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- so they got stranded. They beating because they couldn't jump down. It was quite high and especially when you had luggage in your hand. So this was-- you said you had a muff. It was cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of-- winter of '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: Winter in '42. And this place, I said it, the devil found that place, Auschwitz-Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who beat the women as they came out if they didn't go out fast enough?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who did the beating of the women if they--

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS, the SS man.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw people being beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I said-- I said to the girls, let's run so they haven't got a reason and they can't reach us. But-- when you have to carry and-- and so you can't run. And then they put us in a barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Straight away, they marched-- did-- how did you know to go to a barrack? Did they direct you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they they accompanied us to the barrack. And we went there, dehydrated, tired. And the German woman who came a few days earlier-- they brought up some drink, but they said it's poisoned. And we've been so dehydrated and so thirsty, and that-- that tea, it wasn't real tea. It was a brew made from some leaves. But still, it was warm and it was liquid. So I said, listen, girls. We have to drink. Otherwise we won't last. I am going to drink it. If I am not back in half an hour, then don't drink it. If I come back, how bad it is, you hold your nose and drink it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Come back from where? Where did you have to go to drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Auschwitz. And in Auschwitz, they had toilets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- but you said, if I come back. You were going to drink something. Where were you going to drink from? Where did you have to go to have a drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The kettles was standing before the barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know if I had a-- a dish, what kind of dish I had. Can't-- that I can't remember. But I must have had a dish. I was drinking like you said, with the nose, holding. And and it wasn't poisoned, so I said, girls, we have to drink. And then they brought some food in. Again they said it's poisoned. So again, I said, I'm going to try it. It was rotten-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some-- I don't know how you call it. But what they found, you know, the-- the peasants put that for the winter under the earth. You know, it was a custom that you put over the winter some vegetables or even potatoes under and they cover it with lot of earth. And then they uncover it and it's still edible. But before-- but because the peasants, they wasn't there, the-- the Germans made them run-- go further on and this place was only for them. So the food was under the earth. When they opened that, so it was half-rotten. So that we-- from that, they made the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went and I said, I'm going to try it. Now, it was really terrible. Because I was in the toilet, so here I drank and here I vomited. But I did that a few time, 'til it settled down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came. I said, girls, we have to eat it, because you are hungry, dehydrated. You have to get used to it. So that was our first welcome. And then there was nothing there in that room. So the girls went--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many girls in a room, in the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was 600.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one room.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's a huge room. And so they wanted to undress the coat and put it nicely together. So I said, lie in the coat, because it's more-- you can't-- you will be cold and uncomfortable. Have a look what I will do. I put the muff under my head and I lie in my coat, which had fur collar and fur [INAUDIBLE] and I put my hands in so I shouldn't be so cold. I [INAUDIBLE] I saw that they are very naive and not practical. They taking off the coat. I said, if here we lie, you will have coat. And if not, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's it. Next day, they [NON-ENGLISH], that means 'run, run, run,' beat us and-- so I said, girls, run so you wouldn't be hit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as you were running, the-- the guards would just beat you, would they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then they let us stand. And lot of girls collapse, fainted.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did they let you stand?

speaker: interviewee
text: For hours in the cold. This is-- they-- it wasn't the camp for that, for surviving. It was just for suffering and for killing. So they let us stand there. A lot of girls fainted. And there was another girl from city, Bratislava. She was also a sport girl, so we two had made ourself the-- that they will carry the girls somewhere inside, in a barrack, so they should recover.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the Germans let you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody was there. They-- we were standing, that's what I said. Standing for hours. Nobody said anything. We didn't know what will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards went away.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. After a few hours, when we've been already so tired and-- so they took us to the washroom. We-- they-- we had to undress quickly and naked. And then they put us in big bins, 10 in a very dirty something water. And then naked we came out. Men came and took all the hair from everywhere, naked as we've been. They was laughing. Anyway, and then when we've been half-frozen, so they gave us a remnants of Russian prisoner of war things what left over, which was like a shirt-type and pants. Often the-- the shirt and pants didn't have any button or just one button or so. So sometimes girls had to hold onto it. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do with your clothes? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see what they did with the clothes that you took off?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That-- they took it away. And then they selected and they send that to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got-- what sort of clothing did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of outfit did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: That kind of a-- it was-- it was a green-colored cotton shirt. Mine didn't have a-- a button here. And the pants, I didn't have a button here. So I hold on to it. And later on, we started to dig in the bed or in the mattress, which was very low, and digged in. And I for good luck found two pieces of string, so I straight away had at least something so I didn't have to hold it so strong together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you got your uniforms, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first day, we didn't do anything. They just left us there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the barracks? They left you in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barracks. But then it came, the counting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the morning, they woke us up about 3 o'clock, [NON-ENGLISH] for the counting, because the German woman, there was very primitive prostitutes and thieves and murderers. So they didn't know the counting to do. And the same was the SS woman. It was [? the ?] thousand German woman for the start and a thousand SS woman. And they somehow couldn't count us together. It was not only our barrack. It was more coming. So we had to stand for hours 'til they counted us. Cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is what happened to your first count? You stood-- in your first count, the first time they called you out to count, you had to stand for hours. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that first day, they put us back, because they didn't have the program for us. And then the next day, they was looking for people to work inside, in the kitchen, in the [INAUDIBLE], in the so-called hospital, in the-- where they sew, sew room. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so that was, because they had to establish-- we started a new camp. That's why they-- they had to establish it. But I didn't go nowhere, because I was occupied with four girls who lost their mind for a while. And so I was trying to bring them back to the consciousness that they are not crazy, just a shock what-- what happened. They couldn't believe that it's possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the girls' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the girls' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did remember one, but I've forgotten, so it would take me long to bring her back to memory. But I was very surprised at how good they are now after few days. And so they carried me around and they said, we want Magda, we want Magda, because they were so grateful, because they knew if somebody will found out that they are crazy, then the-- straight away the news went on that they got a phenol injection to the heart and they would be dead in a minute. So now when they found out that I saved their lives, so they were very grateful. And then it happened that the woman in our room-- which was over 600. It was a big room. There was a German, a woman but she was [NON-ENGLISH]. She wasn't from those prostitutes or murderer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was not a Jewish German woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a German.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Jewish German woman. She lived in Warsaw. Yeah. Her husband was a highest medical researcher and doctor. And she was his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- can you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was so much with her together, but I can't remember her name. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she-- she was the one who asked me, why don't you look for-- for a job, like inside job? So I said, I want to go out for agriculture. I want to prepare myself for Palestine, I said. When the war will over-- be over, then we will be able to go to Palestine. So I want to go and prepare myself how-- how to work in-- in agriculture.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she told herself I must be crazy or very naive. So she asked, what are you from profession? So I said, what I am from profession, I don't want ever to be-- to have it here. But still, tell me what it is? I'm a kindergarten teacher. So she said, I am a teacher. So now it became like a bond.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day, when the-- the woman all went to their position what they have to work, so I went to the agriculture colony. So she came and she gave me two [? slaps ?]. And she said, you are coming with me. I need your help.

speaker: interviewer
text: You will be doing cleaning, fetching the food, fetching the-- the tea and the kettles and the bread and everything. So-- and I said, where will I clean the floor and clean the floor? You said you will scratch the dirt with your nails.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I did that-- I told myself, oh, I am not so silly. I'm going to agriculture. And that's why I tried a few time. She slapped me a few times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then she had enough, so she said, what you think that these are agriculture? How long do you think you will last there? They don't want to teach you agriculture? They are just here for killing.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You can keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? So she said, you will be working inside with me and help me, because I can't-- you see my legs are swollen. I can't work so much. So that's-- so I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went for tea. I always speak some girls. I explained to them, we have to go. And I can't carry for 600 people, so who is volunteering? Some did. And if they didn't, so I said, you have to come. So how did you have the power to tell them to come with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just told them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just told them. So you didn't have a position that gave you that power.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I became-- from this woman, she was the [NON-ENGLISH] and I became a [NON-ENGLISH] helper.
